name: Portable SIMD

x.executionModel: A width-agnostic vector type and operations that the compiler lowers to the best instructions for the target ISA, instead of hand-written per-architecture intrinsics.
x.dataOrg: Same SoA / aligned, contiguous data SIMD always wants; the abstraction handles lane width and masking generically.
x.hwConstraints: Bounded by the target ISA’s real capabilities; the abstraction cannot invent hardware features.
x.compilerSupport: Library + compiler lowering: Rust core::simd, C++ std::simd (WD), Highway; .NET Vector<T>; Java Vector API.
x.portability: Its whole purpose: one source compiles to SSE/AVX/NEON/SVE/WASM-SIMD with graceful scaling.
x.expectedBenefit: Vectorisation portability and maintainability without N hand-tuned intrinsic paths.
divergenceRisks: Falling off the optimised path silently
x.workloadShapes: Cross-platform numeric kernels, libraries shipping one codebase to many ISAs.

def: A width-agnostic vector programming model that expresses SIMD once and lets the compiler target each architecture’s instructions, replacing per-ISA intrinsics.
problem: Hand-written intrinsics must be rewritten per architecture (SSE/AVX/NEON/SVE), multiplying code and bugs.
mechanism: Programs use generic vector types and operations (with masks for control flow); the compiler/library selects lane width and lowers to native instructions per target, often with runtime feature dispatch for the best available ISA.
benefits: Far less code than per-arch intrinsics
limits: May trail hand-tuned intrinsics on peak kernels
failures: Silently dropping off the vectorised path
whenNot: A single fixed target where peak intrinsics are worth it
myths: "Portable SIMD is always slower than intrinsics" — it is usually close, and far cheaper to maintain
